1: ʾak ṭôb lĕyisrāʾêl ʾĕlōhîm — truly God is good to Israel. The opening ʾak (truly, surely — an affirmative particle that often concedes a prior doubt) is the key word: this is a conclusion reached through crisis, not an opening axiom of naive faith. The Psalmist has already come through the theodicy problem and now states what he knows. The answer is placed first; the journey to it follows in vv2-27. Lĕbar lēbāb — to those who are pure in heart — the qualification modifies who receives the good: the good of God is for those whose hearts are clear of the deceit that characterized the wicked in v7.
2: waʾănî kimaʿaṭ shāpĕkhû raglāy — as for me, my feet had almost stumbled. The adverb kimaʿaṭ (almost, nearly) marks the near-failure. kĕʾayin shuphĕkhû ʾăshûray — my steps had almost slipped. The verbal root shāpakh (to pour out) applied to feet — as if the legs were liquid, unable to hold. The honesty is stark: the Psalmist came close to apostasy.
3: kî-qinnēʾtî baholĕlîm — for I was envious of the arrogant. Qinnēʾ (piel of qānāʾ: to be zealously envious, to covet) — not mild admiration but burning envy. shĕlôm rĕshāʿîm ʾerʾeh — when I saw the prosperity of the wicked. The theodicy problem is named: the wicked have shālôm (wholeness, ease, prosperity) — what the covenant promised to the righteous. The scandal of their wellbeing is the crisis of the entire psalm.
4: kî ʾên ḥarṣubbôt lĕmôtām — for they have no pangs at their death. Ḥarṣubbôt (bonds, fetters — the pains of dying that bind a person to the dying process) — the wicked die without suffering the torment that afflicts ordinary mortals. ûbārî ʾûlām — and their body is sleek. Bārî (fat, robust, well-fed) — physical prosperity rendered as moral scandal: the wicked are visibly thriving.
5: baʿămal ʾĕnôsh ʾênāmô — they are not in trouble as others are. ʿĀmāl (toil, painful labor, misery) encompasses the entire weight of human struggle under the Adamic curse (cf. Gen 3:17). The wicked float above it. wĕʿim-ʾādām lōʾ yĕnugāʿû — they are not stricken like other men. The universal human condition of suffering (ʾādām: mankind) does not apply to them.
6: lākhēn ʿănāqātmô gāʾăwāh — therefore pride is their necklace. ʿĂnāqāh (necklace, collar — from ʿānaq: to encircle the neck) — pride worn as jewelry, chosen as adornment. yaḥăseph-lāmô ḥāmās — violence covers them as a garment. Violence as a garment wrapping them from head to foot. Both images picture wickedness as self-conscious personal presentation — identity constructed from moral failure, displayed as status.
7: yāṣāʾ mēḥēleb ʿênāmô — their eyes swell out with fatness. The picture is of physical abundance overflowing even into the eyes — overfed, swollen with prosperity. ʿābĕrû maskiyyôt lēbāb — the imaginations of their heart run riot. Maskiyyôt (images, conceits — what the mind pictures to itself) — unchecked fantasies enabled by unchecked prosperity; wealth becomes the permission for inner chaos.
8: yāmîqû wîdabbĕrû bĕrāʿ — they scoff and speak with malice. Mûq (to mock, deride) — the posture of those who have concluded no one will hold them accountable. ʿōshek mimmārôm yĕdabbērû — in their arrogance they threaten oppression. Speaking from the heights — their elevated social position lends coercive force to their words. The powerful can make threats the powerless cannot.
9: shattû vashshāmayim pîhem — they set their mouth against the heavens. Shît (to put, set, place) — their mouths are directed upward in defiance of God. ûlĕshônām tihălakh bāʾāreṣ — and their tongue struts through the earth. The tongue walking (hālakh) through the earth is personification of their speech as an independent agent of domination — the mouth attacks heaven; the tongue conquers earth.
10: lākhēn yāshûb ʿammô hălōm — therefore his people turn back to them. The verse is textually difficult; the most probable reading is that the wicked's success attracts popular following — people are drawn to them. ûmê māleʾ yimmāṣûʾ lāmô — and abundant waters are found by them. The resources and influence flow to those who appear to be winning — the visible success of the wicked distorts the loyalties of ordinary people.
11: wĕʾāmĕrû ʾêkhāh yādaʿ ʾêl — and they say, 'How can God know?' The practical atheism of the prosperous wicked: not denial of God's existence but denial of his attentiveness and moral engagement. wĕyesh daʿat bĕʿelyôn — is there knowledge in the Most High? The rhetorical question assumes the answer: ʿelyôn (El Elyon, the Most High) is ignorant of or indifferent to their affairs. This is the theological claim the sanctuary-vision of v17 refutes.
12: hinnēh ʾēlleh rĕshāʿîm — behold, these are the wicked. The emphatic hinnēh (look!) draws attention to the scandalous exhibit. wĕshallĕwê ʿôlām hirbû-ḥāyil — always at ease, they increase in riches. Shallĕwê ʿôlām (secure, at ease forever) — permanent ease as their condition. The irony: in v18 the Psalmist will reveal that what looks like permanent security is actually a slippery surface.
13: ʾak-rîq zikkîtî lĕbābî — all in vain have I kept my heart clean. Rîq (emptiness, vanity, for nothing) — the faith-crisis moment: righteousness appears meaningless when the wicked flourish. wĕʾerḥaṣ bĕniqyôn kappāy — and washed my hands in innocence. The hand-washing gesture (cf. Ps 26:6) signals ritual purity maintained at personal cost. The Psalmist sees his righteousness as a useless expense.
14: wāʾĕhî nāgûaʿ kol-hayyôm — for all day long I have been stricken. Nāgaʿ (to be struck, afflicted — often of divine discipline) — the righteous one receives what the wicked ought to receive. wĕtôkhḥātî labbĕqārîm — and my rebuke came every morning. Each dawn brings fresh affliction — the righteous man's daily portion is the wicked man's never-portion. The inversion is what makes the theodicy so acute.
15: ʾim-ʾāmartî ʾăsappĕrāh khĕmô — if I had said, 'I will speak thus'. The Psalmist catches himself before speaking the crisis aloud publicly. hinnēh dôr bānêkhā bāgādtî — I would have betrayed the generation of your children. The restraint is moral and social: the teacher's public declaration can destabilize the faith of the community. This is not suppression of honest doubt but the redirection of it — from public broadcast to private audience with God (v17). The burden of faith-formation constrains the freedom of the theologian's complaint.
16: wāʾăḥashshĕbāh lādaʿat zōʾt — when I thought about this. The verb ḥāshab (to reckon, plan, compute — the accountant's word) — the Psalmist tried to reason his way through the theodicy problem rationally. ʿāmāl hîʾ bĕʿênāy — it was a wearisome task in my sight. ʿĀmāl (toil, painful effort) — intellectual resolution proved as exhausting as the problem itself. The mind cannot reason its way to peace; a different kind of knowledge is required.
17: ʿad-ʾābôʾ ʾel-miqdĕshê-ʾêl — until I came into the sanctuary of God. The structural pivot of the entire psalm — everything before is the crisis, everything after is the resolution. The sanctuary provides what reasoning could not: an eschatological vantage point, a perspective from which the temporal success of the wicked looks very different. ʾābînāh lĕʾaḥărîtām — I discerned their end. ʾAḥărît (end, latter end, final state) — the sanctuary reveals not the current state of the wicked but their end. Wisdom begins where human calculation stops.
18: ʾak baḥălāqôt tāshît lāmô — truly you set them in slippery places. Ḥălāqôt (slippery places — from ḥālaq: to be smooth, to flatter) — they appear stable but stand on ice. hippaltām lĕmashuʾôt — you cast them down to ruin. Mashuʾôt (ruins, desolation) — what appeared permanent prosperity is momentary before the divine action already in motion. The sanctuary-view reveals what ordinary time conceals: the wicked are already falling.
19: ʾêkh hāyû lĕshammāh kĕrāgaʿ — how they are destroyed in a moment. Rāgaʿ (a moment, an instant) — the apparent permanence of v12 (shallĕwê ʿôlām, secure forever) collapses in a single moment. sāpû tammû min-ballāhôt — they are swept away utterly by terrors. Ballāhôt (sudden terrors, panic — from bālah: to terrify, consume) — the end they denied comes as the very terror they believed could not reach them.
20: kĕḥălôm mēhāqîṣ ʾădōnāy — like a dream when one awakes, O Lord. The simile is devastating: the wicked's entire reality is like a dream that has no substance in the daylight. bĕʿîr ṣalmām tibzeh — when you arise you will despise their phantoms. Ṣalmām (their image, shadow-form) — they are mere shadow-images in God's sight; his arising disperses them as dawn disperses dreams. God's waking is the Last Judgment.
21: kî yithĕmamēr lĕbābî — when my heart was embittered. Ḥāmar (to ferment, boil up, be embittered — the same root as ḥāmēṣ, leavened bread that has fermented to excess) — the interior state was fermenting with unprocessed bitterness. wĕkîlyôtay ʾeshtonān — and I was pierced in my kidneys. Kîlyôt (kidneys) is the deep interior of the body, the seat of emotion and moral sense in Hebrew physiology (cf. Ps 16:7; Prov 23:16). The pain was visceral, not merely intellectual.
22: waʾănî-baʿar wĕlōʾ ʾēdāʿ — I was brutish and ignorant. Baʿar (brutish, stupid — from the root of livestock, bĕhēmāh) — the Psalmist's self-description at the height of the crisis: animal-level comprehension. bĕhēmôt hāyîtî ʿimmāk — I was like a beast before you. He was in the presence of God — in the sanctuary of v17 — yet behaving like an animal that cannot perceive divine reality. Proximity to God without understanding is the condition of the beast.
23: waʾănî tāmîd ʿimmāk — nevertheless I am continually with you. The pivot from self-condemnation to affirmation — the tāmîd (continually, always) of God's presence as the stable reality even during the crisis, even during the bestial incomprehension of v22. āḥazta bîyad-yĕmînî — you hold me by my right hand. Not the Psalmist grasping God but God grasping the Psalmist — the grip is from the divine side, maintained even when the human side was failing.
24: bĕʿaṣātekhā tanḥēnî — you guide me with your counsel. Divine guidance through ʿēṣāh (counsel, advice, wisdom) — not fate but relational wisdom-sharing, as between friends or a father and son. wĕʾaḥar kābôd tiqḥēnî — and afterward you will receive me into glory. Aḥar (afterward, after this life) + kābôd (glory, the weighty divine presence) + tiqḥēnî (you will take me, receive me — the qal of lāqaḥ used of Enoch's translation in Gen 5:24 and Elijah's in 2 Kgs 2:3,9). This is one of the OT's clearest intimations of post-death communion with God: the receiving into glory is the answer to the wicked's end in ruin (v18).
25: mî-lî bashshāmāyim — whom do I have in heaven but you? A radical personal monotheistic confession: all heavenly beings, all divine candidates, all possible intercessors in the heavenly realm are set at naught. wĕʿimmĕkhā lōʾ-ḥāpaṣtî bāʾāreṣ — and there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you. The complete negation of desire for anything earthly — wealth, health, the prosperity of the wicked in v12 — in favor of God alone. The very prosperity the Psalmist once envied is now explicitly renounced.
26: kālāh shĕʾērî ûlĕbābî — my flesh and my heart may fail. Kālāh (to be finished, consumed, spent) — even the body and the innermost self may give out. But: ṣûr-lĕbābî wĕḥelqî ʾĕlōhîm lĕʿôlām — God is the rock of my heart and my portion forever. Ṣûr (rock) applied to the heart itself — the usual epithet of God as external fortress now internalized. Ḥēleq (portion, lot, allotment) — the Levitical term (Num 18:20: YHWH as the Levites' inheritance). The Psalmist adopts the Levitical identity: God himself is his inheritance, not land or wealth.
27: kî-hinnēh rĕḥêqêkhā yōbēdû — for behold, those who are far from you shall perish. The rĕḥêqîm (those far away) are the wicked of the entire psalm — their distance from God is not incidental to their ruin but constitutive of it. To be far from God is to be in the place of perishing. hiṣmattāh kol-zôneh mimmekā — you silence all who are unfaithful to you. Zānāh (to commit harlotry, to be faithlessly unfaithful) — the wicked's practical atheism (v11) is spiritual adultery; they live as if wedded to success rather than to God.
28: waʾănî qirbat ʾĕlōhîm lî-ṭôb — but for me it is good to be near God. Qirbāh (nearness, drawing near — a cultic term for access to the sanctuary, used in priestly legislation) — the answer to the entire theodicy is not an explanation but a relationship. The goodness of v1 (ʾak ṭôb lĕyisrāʾêl ʾĕlōhîm) becomes personal: lî-ṭôb (for me it is good). shattî baʾădōnāy YHWH maḥsî — I have made the Lord GOD my refuge. The pair ʾădōnāy YHWH (Lord GOD) closes the psalm of Book III's opening with sovereign weight.
